coredata.dc:description: To extend the photo-response range and quantum efficiency of titanium dioxide (TiO2), it was sensitized with lignin-derived red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs). The RCDs consisted of a base lignin structure and many types of functional groups (e.g., CO, -NH2, -OSO3H, -NR2, and -OH). The optimal emission wavelength of the RCDs was 628 nm, with a fluorescence lifetime of 8.56 ns. The TiO2 photocatalyst particles were sensitized by RCDs, which narrowed the band gap of TiO2 and increased the efficiency of carrier photogeneration. The RCDs/TiO2 possessed a double band-gap structure with the band-gap energies of 1.60 and 3.13 eV. The photocurrent response of RCDs/TiO2 under simulated solar irradiation were 1.2 to 4.5 μA·cm−2. The sensitization of TiO2 by RCDs facilitated the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. Under simulated sunlight, the degradation of tetracycline by RCDs/TiO2 reached 95.0 % and the mineralization ratio was 57.5 % within 60 min.

head.ce:title: Lignin-derived red carbon dots sensitized TiO2 photocatalysts for tetracycline degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To extend the photo-response range and quantum efficiency of titanium dioxide (TiO2), it was sensitized with lignin-derived red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs). The RCDs consisted of a base lignin structure and many types of functional groups (e.g., CO, -NH2, -OSO3H, -NR2, and -OH). The optimal emission wavelength of the RCDs was 628 nm, with a fluorescence lifetime of 8.56 ns. The TiO2 photocatalyst particles were sensitized by RCDs, which narrowed the band gap of TiO2 and increased the efficiency of carrier photogeneration. The RCDs/TiO2 possessed a double band-gap structure with the band-gap energies of 1.60 and 3.13 eV. The photocurrent response of RCDs/TiO2 under simulated solar irradiation were 1.2 to 4.5 μA·cm−2. The sensitization of TiO2 by RCDs facilitated the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. Under simulated sunlight, the degradation of tetracycline by RCDs/TiO2 reached 95.0 % and the mineralization ratio was 57.5 % within 60 min.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials composed of sp2- or sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. CDs have been widely used in various fields, including catalysis [1,2], bioimaging [3,4], optical detection [5], and sensing [6,7]. The raw materials for CDs include inorganic carbon sources, such as graphite [8], carbon nanotubes [9], activated carbon [10], and candle ash [11], and organic carbon sources, such as glucose [12], polyethylene glycol [13], bagasse [14], watermelon rind [15], amino acids [16], and citric acid [17]. In recent years, biomass has also been used to prepare CDs because of its wide range of sources and large reserves. Biomass carbon sources for CDs include cellulose [18], hemicellulose [19], lignin [20], and herbs [21].

Lignin is a major component of lignocellulosic biomass, and its natural aromatic nuclear structure makes it a suitable material for the preparation of CDs. The cross-linking of individual units makes the lignin structure relatively stable. Chen et al. [22] prepared CDs using lignin as a carbon source. Subsequently, alkali lignin [23] and lignin sulfonate [24] were used as raw materials for CDs preparation. The emission color of such CDs prepared without additives is predominantly blue. Adding acid (such as phosphoric acid [25], sulfuric acid [25], or nitric acid [26]) or base (such as ammonia [27] or ethylenediamine [28]) during the CDs preparation process with lignin as the carbon source can effectively increase the CDs quantum yield and alter emission color. In particular, acids or bases containing N or S can markedly change the emission color of CDs. CDs typically appear green, cyan, or yellow under ultraviolet (UV) excitation; lignin-based red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs) are rare. The production and application of RCDs are limited. Therefore, it is important to synthesize lignin-based RCDs.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is widely regarded as a promising material in the field of wastewater treatment due to its high stability and excellent catalytic properties. However, its inherent wide band gap (3.20 eV) restricts it to absorbing only UV light during photocatalysis. UV light accounts for just 5 % of solar light, which seriously affects he solar-based applications of TiO2. Furthermore, the inefficient generation of electron–hole pairs by TiO2 hinders its overall catalytic performance. To address these limitations of TiO2, it is often composited with other materials such as precious metals [29], semiconductors [30], biomass [31], or carbon materials [32]. In particular, considerable work has been conducted on the composites of TiO2 with carbon materials. TiO2 is often modified with carbon materials such as activated carbon [32], carbon nanotubes [33], graphene [34], or CDs [35]. In recent years, CDs have been composited with various semiconductors, including C3N4 [36], CdS [37], ZnO [38], and BiVO4 [39]. Such composites have a wide range of applications in environmental pollution control [36], energy conversion [39], and chemical synthesis [40]. The addition of CDs to semiconductors can greatly improve the separation efficiency of charge carriers and enhance catalytic efficiency. Many studies have been carried out on the modification of TiO2 with CDs, mostly ones that emit blue [41] or green [42] light. However, there are few studies on the modification of TiO2 with CDs with long emission wavelengths. Such CDs, which include RCDs, have the advantages of high luminescence stability, adjustable band gap, and large two-photon absorption cross section. Therefore, CDs with long emission wavelength hold certain advantages for enhancing the catalytic performance of TiO2.

Starting with the natural aromatic structure of lignin, we use a one-step method to prepare RCDs. The fluorescence mechanism of the RCDs is elucidated by evaluating their structure, composition, and optical properties. The RCDs are then used to sensitize TiO2. The effect of the RCDs on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 is assessed using tetracycline (TC) [30,43] as a model target under simulated solar irradiation. Modification of the TiO2 photocatalyst with RCDs broadens its light response range, increases the separation efficiency of charge carriers, and enhances the degradation of TC.

Ammonium sulfate ((NH4)2SO4), o-phenylenediamine, ascorbic acid and sodium sulfate were purchased by Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Tianjin, China). Ammonia (NH3·H2O) and anhydrous ethanol are produced by Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Tianjin, China). Titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4), Tetracycline hydrochloride, Methyl violet and Lignin (Dealkaline) were produced by Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were produced by Tianjin Yaohua Chemical Reagent Co., LTD., (Tianjin, China). The above reagents were analytically pure. Filter paper is produced by Hangzhou Special Paper Co. LTD. (Hangzhou, China).

Dealkaline lignin (0.1 g) and o-phenylenediamine (0.2 g) were added to a reaction vessel with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and then 40 wt% H2SO4 (15 mL) was added. The mixture was placed in a homogeneous reactor and heated at 220 °C for 10 h to form RCDs. The cooled reaction mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was poured into a 500 M dialysis bag and dialyzed for 24 h. The water was changed every 12 h during dialysis. The resulting suspension contained a mass concentration of RCDs of 0.40 wt%.
The literature method was used to synthesize [44].

TiO2 (0.5 g) was added to a suspension containing RCDs (0.025 g, 0.4 wt%). Absolute ethanol (10 mL) was added and then the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then the supernatant was removed. The solid was collected from the centrifuge tube and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h to give the RCDs/TiO2 catalyst (Scheme 1 ).

Fourier infrared transform (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer, TV 1900, USA) was used to observe the surface composition of the RCDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi, America) was conducted using Al-Kα radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV). Binding energies were calibrated to the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. Photoluminescence spectra of RCDs were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., LS55, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence decay curves of RCDs were recorded using a different fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., QM8000, Edison, NJ, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations of RCDs and RCDs/TiO2 samples were carried out using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, Japan). The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of RCDs/TiO2 was collected at −195.85 °C using an adsorption analyzer (ASAP 2460). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) diffuse emission spectra of RCDs/TiO2 were measured using a UV–Vis–near-infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, 3600-plus, Japan) with barium sulfate as a reference. Electrochemical analyses were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660B, CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai, China). Mott–Schottky and transient photocurrent response measurements of RCDs/TiO2 were performed using a three-electrode setup. TC degradation and RCDs were observed by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, Beijing Pulse General Instrument Co., Ltd. Beijing, China).

Photocatalytic degradation reactions were carried out in a photocatalytic reactor under a 300-W xenon lamp (CEL-PF300–16, Beijing China Education AuLight Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) with a light intensity of ~100 mW cm−2 at the surface of the photocatalyst. The catalyst (0.5 g/L) was added to aqueous TC solution (0.01 g/L). The reaction mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. During photodegradation, an aliquot (3.00 mL) was collected every 10 min with a syringe and filtered through a 0.22-μm organic filtration membrane. The absorbance of TC was measured at λ = 358 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, Beijing Pulse General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The mineralization ratio for the TC degradation process was analyzed using a total organic carbon analyzer (Shimadzu, TOC-L, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and elemental analysis of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) and (b) reveals that the RCDs were well-dispersed and quasi-spherical with a diameter of approximately 3.0 nm. Lattice fringes can be clearly seen in the high-resolution TEM image; the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [45]. This proved that the RCDs were crystalline. Fig. 1(c) shows the FT-IR spectra of the dealkaline lignin starting material and RCDs. Compared with lignin, the RCDs contained many different functional groups. The peak at 3400 cm−1 is the -OH stretching vibration of lignin, while that at 1270 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CO in the guaiacol-type structural unit of lignin [46]. The peaks at 1138 and 1034 cm−1 were attributed to the CH stretching vibration and aromatic CH in-plane bending vibration in the guaiacol-type structure of lignin, respectively. The peaks at 855 and 815 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CH in the bulk benzene ring structure [46]. The RCDs displayed broad peaks in the 3700–2400 cm−1 region. We speculate that these peaks were caused by tensile vibration of OH, telescopic vibration of NH, and telescopic vibration of OH. The expansion vibration of NH and expansion vibration of OH are associated, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonds. These interactions also endow the RCDs with hydrophilic properties. The RCDs exhibited new characteristic peaks ascribed to the conjugated CC expansion vibration at 1615 cm−1, NC expansion vibration/N-H deformation vibration at 1516 cm−1, SO symmetrical expansion vibration at 1140 cm−1, and SO expansion vibration at 1020 cm−1 [24,47,48]. The FT-IR spectral analysis indicated that a large amount of the basic structure of lignin was retained in the RCDs. However, the RCDs possessed more N- and S-containing functional groups than the lignin starting material. This spectral analysis proved that N- and S-co-doped RCDs were prepared from lignin.

XPS was used to analyze the state of the elements in the RCDs. Fig. S1 shows the survey spectrum of the RCDs, which contains O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p signals. Fig. 1(d)–(g) presents high-resolution XPS plots of the RCDs. C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p peaks appeared at 284.8, 532.0, 401.7, and 169.2 eV, respectively. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(d) was deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to C=C/C-C (284.8 eV), C-N/C-S (286.3 eV), and CO (288.2 eV) signals. The O 1s profile in Fig. 1(e) was deconvoluted into S=O/S-O/C-O (532.1 eV) and CO (534.1 eV) signals. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 1(f) contained peaks from N-C/N=C (401.7 eV) and H-N-(C)2 (400.3 eV) [49]. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 1(g) was deconvoluted into two peaks; CS and S=O/S-O. As shown in Table S1, the C-C/C=C percentage of C in the RCDs was 78.2 %. In conjunction with our previous work [50], we speculate that the addition of H2SO4 promoted the charring of lignin and increased doping with elemental S [51,52]. This is because lignin is first partially hydrolyzed by H2SO4 as the reaction temperature rises, which increases the graphitization of the RCDs. In addition, the O content of the RCDs was as high as 40.72 % (Table S1), which is considered to be an important reason for their long emission wavelength [53]. The results of FT-IR and XPS analyses indicate that the presence of H2SO4 during the hydrothermal process caused β-O-4 in lignin to begin to break down [54], which decreased the molecular weight of lignin as compounds with low molecular weight (aromatic acids, phenols, and aromatic ethers) began to form [55]. In addition, numerous positively charged carbon ions are generated during this process [55,56]. These electrophilic carbocations can react with the aromatic phenyl rings of lignin, o-phenylenediamine, and H2SO4; meanwhile, the stable, electron-rich carbon atoms react to form aromatic fragments [55,56]. In this process, o-phenylenediamine and H2SO4 promote the formation N- and S-containing functional groups on the surface of the RCDs (Scheme 2 ). The formation of RCDs entails numerous decomposition and polymerization processes.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the RCDs displayed absorption peaks at 267, 273, 563, and 612 nm in their UV–Vis spectrum. The previous analysis indicated that the RCDs have numerous chromophoric (e.g., CC, CO, and CN) and co-chromophoric (e.g., -NH2, -OSO3H, -NR2, and -OH) functional groups on their surface. The interaction of the chromophoric functional groups with the aromatic structures in the RCDs led to the occurrence of π-π* transitions. Therefore, the peaks at 263 and 273 nm should be the π-π* transitions in the K and B bands, respectively. The absorption peaks at 563 and 612 nm may originate from the non-bonding electrons of the heteroatoms in CO and CN π* transitioning to the anti-bonding orbitals; that is, n-π* transitions. Alternatively, they may be related to the presence of π-conjugated electrons in the sp2 structural domains and connected surface groups/polymer chains. To investigate the origin of the fluorescence of the RCDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of the RCDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 2(a) reveals that the RCDs had a maximum excitation wavelength of 602 nm and maximum emission wavelength of 628 nm. Moreover, Fig. 2(b) illustrates that the emission wavelength of the RCDs did not change with increasing emission wavelength, but the fluorescence emission intensity did change. Fluorescence emission involves a single transition that requires a well-defined excitation energy and thus does not depend on excitation wavelength [57].

The fluorescence lifetime of the RCDs was measured (Fig. 2(c)). The emission decay curve of RCDs was fitted to a biexponential function (Table S2). This indicates the presence of two types of fluorophores in RCDs; namely, graphitized carbon core and surface fluorophores. The average lifetime of the RCDs was calculated to be 8.56 ns. Under the 602-nm excitation, the quantum yield of the RCDs was 5.26 %. The numerous chromophores on the RCD surface contributed to the redshift of their emission wavelength. The addition of H2SO4 to the lignin starting material increased the production of aromatic fragments, the incorporation of N and S, and O content of the RCDs, all of which contributed to the emission behavior of the RCDs. The emission wavelength of the RCDs was longer than 600 nm, so the RCDs displayed red emission under the visible-light excitation, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and elemental analyses of RCDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3(a) shows that the surface morphology of RCDs/TiO2 was layered. The region highlighted within the red square in Fig. 3(a) is depicted with greater detail in Fig. 3(b), revealing a distinct lattice structure of RCDs/TiO2.The lattice spacing was 0.352 nm, which corresponds to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2. This indicates that the composite formation process did not change the crystal structure of TiO2. A lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of graphite, was also identified. Surface scanning was performed on the area in the yellow square in Fig. 3(a); the results are presented in Fig. 3(c). The RCDs/TiO2 contained C, O, S, and Ti. The low content of elemental N in the RCDs made it difficult to observe elemental N in RCDs/TiO2.

The surface chemical composition and elemental chemical states of the prepared composite catalysts were further analyzed by XPS. The survey spectra in Fig. 4(a) show that TiO2 consists of C, O, and Ti and RCDs/TiO2 consists of C, O, S, and Ti. The C 1s peak of TiO2 (Fig. 4(b)) originated from adventitious C. The C 1s spectrum of the RCDs/TiO2 was fitted with the same peaks as those in the C 1s spectrum of the RCDs. However, the peak positions were shifted because of composite formation with TiO2. The O 1s spectra in Fig. 4(c) show that TiO2 contained oxygen vacancies (531.4 eV) and TiO (530.2 eV) bonds [58]. Meanwhile, RCDs/TiO2 displayed O 1s signals at 530.9 and 531.6 eV that were attributed to TiO and S-O/C-O bonds, respectively [59,60].

The Ti 2p spectrum of TiO2 showed peaks at 459.0 and 464.8 eV that were attributed to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4(d)). The Ti 2p spectrum of RCDs/TiO2 displayed peaks at 457.8 and 463.6 eV that were assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4(d)). Doping with RCDs caused the binding energy of Ti 2p to decrease. This may be because, on the one hand, the introduction of RCDs led to the conversion of Ti4+ to Ti3+ [61]. On the other hand, it may result from C atoms entering the TiO2 lattice and forming Ti-C-O bonds, which increased the electron density around the Ti and O atoms and decreased the binding energy [62]. The binding energies of RCDs/TiO2 were decreased compared with those of TiO2; e.g., their Ti 2p1/2 binding energies differed by 1.2 eV. The formation of Ti-C-O bonds proved that the RCDs formed a composite with TiO2. The elemental content of Ti in RCDs/TiO2 was 25.87 %, as shown in Table S1. The elemental content of O in RCDs/TiO2 was 61.14 %, which was much higher than the theoretical O content of TiO2, indicating that the RCDs formed a composite with TiO2. The elemental content of S in RCDs/TiO2 was only 1.71 %, and N could not be detected because of its low doping content.

The addition of RCDs did not change the crystalline phase of TiO2 (Fig. 5(a)), but the intensity of the diffraction peaks weakened to different degrees following composite formation. This was caused by the dilution effect of the amorphous RCDs. The characteristic peaks of the RCDs were not observed in the XRD pattern of RCDs/TiO2, which is because they were masked by the much stronger peaks of TiO2. The UV–Vis spectra in Fig. 5(b) reveal that the transmittance of TiO2 decreased after the formation of RCDs/TiO2, especially in the range of 580 to 620 nm. This was caused by the optical properties of the RCDs. According to the Kubelka–Munk formula, the energy band gap of TiO2 is 3.20 eV (Fig. 5(c)). Surprisingly, we found that RCDs/TiO2 had a double band-gap structure with band gaps of 1.60 and 3.13 eV, consistent with a previous report [63]. The presence of RCDs generated impurity energy levels in TiO2, which narrowed its band gap, thus increasing its absorption of visible light [61]. The RCDs themselves have excitation and emission wavelengths in the visible region, so composite formation changes the forbidden bandwidth of TiO2 and allows it to absorb red light. Overall, the formation of a composite between RCDs and TiO2 changes the bandwidth of TiO2 and increases its light utilization efficiency.

Adsorption–desorption curves of the photocatalysts are presented in Fig. 5(d). Both TiO2 and RCDs/TiO2 exhibited type-IV curves with H3-type hysteresis loops. Table S3 reveals that the specific surface area of RCDs/TiO2 was almost the same as that of TiO2. The pore size distributions of RCDs/TiO2 and TiO2 were similar (Fig. S3), but pore volume decreased after composite formation. The valence band spectra of TiO2 and RCDs/TiO2 are presented in Fig. 5(e). Tangent analysis of the edges of the valence band spectra showed that the valence band energies (EVB) of TiO2 and RCDs/TiO2 were 2.02 and 2.16 eV, respectively. The EVB values were used to determine conduction band energies (ECB) according to Eq. (1),
(1)
E g
=
E VB
−
E CB
where Eg is the forbidden bandwidth. The calculated ECB values were −1.18 eV for TiO2 and 0.56 eV and −0.97 eV for RCDs/TiO2. Under continuous illumination switching, the transient photocurrent of RCDs/TiO2 was much higher than that of TiO2. This is because the RCDs promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and broadened the spectral response range of TiO2. Indicated by the photocurrent, a higher concentration of photogenerated electrons on the photocatalyst surface should be favorable to improve photocatalytic performance [64,65]. The photocurrent of RCDs/TiO2 is much higher than that of TiO2 (Fig. 5(f)). This is because the stronger absorption of light by RCDs increased the overall current response of RCDs/TiO2. Fig. S4 shows the Mott–Schottky curves of RCDs/TiO2 and TiO2. The linear parts have positive slopes and the addition of RCDs did not change the semiconductor properties of TiO2. TiO2 and RCDs/TiO2 displayed flat band potentials of −0.45 and −0.38 eV, respectively. The addition of RCDs shifted the flat band potential in the positive direction, which means that the conduction band of RCDs/TiO2 shifted in the positive direction relative to that of TiO2 [66].

The effectiveness of degrading various concentrations of TC using TiO2 and RCDs/TiO2 is illustrated in Fig. S5(a). The most efficient degradation occurred at a TC concentration of 10 mg/L, achieving 95 % degradation within 60 min. However, as the TC concentration increased, the efficiency of degradation gradually diminished. The TC degradation process conformed to the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation in Fig. S5(b). At a TC concentration of 10 mg/L, the rate constant (k) was 0.0465, and when the TC concentration was increased to 160 mg/L, k was only 0.00818. This trend is consistent with that of the degradation efficiency with rising TC concentration. Solutions with high TC concentration absorbed more incident light, which lowered the photon flux received by RCDs/TiO2, decreasing its light utilization.

We selected 10 mg/L (pH = 6.0) as the substrate concentration for degradation and investigated the photocatalytic activity of RCDs/TiO2 under different light sources. Fig. 6(a) and (b) shows that under the UV light source, the degradation of TC by TiO2 reached 97.4 % in 50 min with k = 0.0841. The degradation of TC by RCDs/TiO2 reached 94.1 % after 60 min with k = 0.0545. The photocatalytic effect of TiO2 was stronger than that of RCDs/TiO2 under UV irradiation. However, under simulated solar light conditions, as shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d), the degradation of TC by RCDs/TiO2 reached 95 % within 60 min with k = 0.0493. Conversely, TiO2 had almost no degradation effect on TC within 60 min under simulated sunlight. Comparing the results in Table 1 reveals that RCDs/TiO2 have a relatively high ability to degrade TC under different light conditions. Based on the good performance of the catalyst, we perform the degradation of tetracycline under real sunlight (Fig. S7). The degradation ratio of RCDs/TiO2 to TC reached 96.2 % in 120 min (Table S3).

Considering practical application value, the RCDs/TiO2 photocatalyst is more suitable for practical application than TiO2. Under a UV light source, RCDs/TiO2 showed a weaker photocatalytic effect than TiO2. This is because both RCDs and TiO2 produce electrons under the high-energy UV excitation, so the escaping electrons may undergo interparticle complexation, which decreases their utilization efficiency. Under simulated solar light, the excitation energy is greatly lowered compared with that under UV light. In this case, we speculate that the electrons in the valence band of the RCDs are excited into the conduction band. The unexcited electrons in the RCDs are transferred to the empty conduction band of TiO2, allowing it to participate in the catalytic reaction. The RCDs/TiO2 photocatalyst absorbs more light than TiO2 alone; in addition to its the absorption below 380 nm, it also absorbs light around 700 nm in the visible region. This results in more photogenerated carriers and the higher catalytic efficiency of RCDs/TiO2 than that of TiO2. Therefore, the catalytic efficiency of the composite catalyst is obviously affected by electron transfer. This is consistent with the results of transient photocurrent measurements.

To investigate the effect of pH on the photocatalytic properties of the prepared RCDs/TiO2 composite, TC degradation under different pH conditions was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7(a), pH influenced the photocatalytic efficiency of RCDs/TiO2. The highest degradation of TC was observed in the pH interval of 5.0 to 9.0. With the increase of acidity or alkalinity outside this range, the ability of RCDs/TiO2 to degrade TC gradually weakened. These results indicate that RCDs/TiO2 should not be used in strongly acidic or alkaline environments. Cycling experiments were then conducted on RCDs/TiO2 to investigate its reusability (Fig. 7(b)). After 6 cycles, the degradation ratio of TC was still 84 %, indicating that the RCDs/TiO2 catalyst has good stability. We speculate that the reason for the decrease in TC degradation efficiency with increasing cycle number is caused by the possible loss of RCDs during the cycling process. As a result, the electrons become slower in the transport process, so the degradation effect of RCDs/TiO2 weakened. Table S1 and Fig. S6 show that the O content of RCDs/TiO2 decreased after cycling. This behavior is consistent with our speculation that some RCDs were lost during catalyst reuse.

To investigate the roles of active substances in the TC degradation process over the composite catalyst, we selected methyl viologen as a trapping agent of super-oxide radicals [73], ethanol as a trapping agent of hydroxide ions [74], NaN3 as a trapping agent of singlet oxygen [75], KBrO3 as a trapping agent of electrons [76], and ascorbic acid as a trapping agent of holes [77]. The results in Fig. 7(c) show that the addition of ascorbic acid had the greatest effect on the degradation of TC. Therefore, holes played a crucial role in TC degradation by RCDs/TiO2.

The biocompatibility of the TC solution after degradation was investigated through the cultivation of Arabidopsis thaliana seeds [67]. To this end, some Arabidopsis thaliana seeds were cultured in tap water (serving as the control sample), tap water containing TC (untreated solution), and photo-catalytically treated TC solution. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the seeds exhibited minimal growth in the untreated solution. Conversely, the seeds demonstrated considerable growth in the photo-catalytically treated solution, approaching the level observed in the control sample. Therefore, the TC solution after degradation by RCDs/TiO2 possesses considerable cultivation capacity.

The interaction between RCDs and TiO2 enhanced the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 in TC decomposition under simulated solar irradiation. This effect can mainly be explained by light absorption, charge separation, surface reactions, and degradation pathways. The photocatalytic process begins with the absorption of light. The RCDs/TiO2 photocatalyst possesses two different bandgaps of 1.60 and 3.13 eV, which absorb visible light from the solar spectrum. Under light excitation, electrons in RCDs/TiO2 are excited to the conduction band and holes remain in the valence band. The electrons in the conduction band are easily transferred to a special energy level. Staggered energy levels greatly contribute to the efficiency of charge separation [78,79]. Electrons and holes generate reactive oxygen species, which attack the TC molecules adsorbed on the RCDs/TiO2 surface, resulting in the cleavage of chemical bonds to generate species with lower molecular weight. Eventually, these species are completely mineralized to CO2 and H2O [80] (Fig. 9 ).

The RCDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using lignin as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source. Structural characterization revealed that the graphitization degree, numerous conjugated structures, and N- and S- co-doping allowed the RCDs to emit red light. The RCDs had little effect on the morphology and pore structure of TiO2, but markedly narrowed its band gap and increased its photogenerated carrier transport efficiency. Under simulated solar irradiation, the degradation of TC over RCDs/TiO2 reached 95.0 % and the mineralization ratio was 57.5 % after 60 min. Holes (h+) were considered to be an important factor influencing the TC degradation process. The RCDs/TiO2 photocatalyst had application potential, achieving a 96.2 % degradation of TC within 120 min under real sun.
